g.data-narration: Welcome to the Multi-Armed Bandit Problem - a fundamental concept in reinforcement learning and decision theory.
Imagine walking into a casino with multiple slot machines - each with unknown reward probabilities. This is the MAB problem!
g.text: A fundamental RL problem where an agent must choose between K arms
with unknown reward probabilities, balancing exploration and exploitation.

g.data-narration: The core dilemma: exploration tries new options to learn, exploitation uses the best-known option. Both are essential!
g.text: Trying different arms to learn reward probabilities
Choosing arm with highest observed reward

g.data-narration: Formally, we have K arms with unknown reward distributions. At each time step, we select an arm and receive a reward.
g.text: • Reward distribution: Each arm i has unknown mean μ_i and variance σ²_i
• Time horizon: T rounds to maximize cumulative reward
Goal: Maximize Σ r_t over T rounds

g.data-narration: Epsilon-Greedy is the simplest approach: with probability ε explore randomly, otherwise exploit the best-known arm.
g.text: With probability ε: explore (select random arm)
With probability 1-ε: exploit (select arm with max Q(a))
Q(a) = average reward from arm a

g.data-narration: The algorithm flow: generate random number, compare with epsilon, then either explore randomly or exploit the best arm.
UCB adds an exploration bonus based on uncertainty. Less-explored arms get higher bonus, encouraging natural exploration.
g.text: UCB adds an exploration bonus based on uncertainty:

g.data-narration: The second term in UCB is the exploration bonus - it decreases as we learn more but is larger for less-explored arms.
g.text: 🔑 UCB: How the Exploration Bonus Works

g.data-narration: Thompson Sampling is a Bayesian approach: maintain probability distributions over arm rewards and sample to select.
g.text: A Bayesian method that maintains probability distributions over arm rewards:

g.data-narration: For binary rewards, we use Beta distributions. The distribution narrows as we get more data, representing increased certainty.
MAB is a special case of RL. Different problem types vary by information available - from no state to fully observable environments.
MAB has diverse applications: clinical trials, online advertising, recommendation systems, and A/B testing all use bandit algorithms.
Epsilon-Greedy is simple but wasteful. UCB has theoretical guarantees. Thompson Sampling offers best practical performance.
In summary: MAB balances exploration and exploitation. Epsilon-Greedy is simple, UCB has theory, Thompson Sampling excels in practice.
g.text: • MAB is the foundation of RL - balancing explore vs exploit
• Epsilon-Greedy is simple but doesn't distinguish exploration quality
• UCB has optimal regret bounds, exploration bonus decreases with experience
• Thompson Sampling excels in practice via Bayesian posterior sampling

g.data-narration: Understanding these algorithms and their tradeoffs is essential for any reinforcement learning practitioner. Happy learning!
g.text: Understanding MAB provides the foundation for all sequential decision-making under uncertainty!
